title: Modelling — graphical, physical and CAD
summary: This lesson explores the crucial role of modelling in the design process. It covers the types, purposes, advantages, and disadvantages of graphical, physical, and Computer-Aided Design (CAD) models, equipping students to select and justify the most appropriate modelling technique for a given design context.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between graphical, physical, and CAD models, explaining their respective advantages and disadvantages.
Evaluate the role of modelling for testing and evaluating design ideas within a specific context.
Justify the selection of a particular modelling technique in relation to the design development process.
Understand the difference between mock-ups and prototypes, and the concepts of fidelity and scale.

simpleExplanation.title: From Idea to Reality: The Designer's Toolkit
simpleExplanation.summary: Models are simplified versions of a design used to explore, test, and communicate ideas before committing to expensive production. They can be anything from a quick sketch on a napkin to a fully interactive digital simulation.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you're building a custom treehouse. You'd first sketch the design to figure out the shape and where the windows go (a graphical model). Then, you might build a small version out of cardboard boxes to check its stability and layout (a physical model). Finally, you could use a computer program to create a detailed 3D plan, ensuring all the wooden beams fit perfectly and calculating the total cost (a CAD model). Each step helps you avoid mistakes and perfect the final treehouse.
steps: First, identify the purpose of the model. Are you trying to communicate a basic concept, test how a user interacts with the product, or analyse its structural integrity?
Next, select the appropriate model type. Choose between graphical (drawings), physical (mock-ups, prototypes), or CAD based on your purpose, budget, and timeline.

Then, construct the model with the required level of detail, or 'fidelity'. A quick foam block might be enough for early ergonomic tests, while a high-fidelity 3D print is better for a final presentation.
Finally, use the model to test and gather feedback. Analyse the results to iterate and improve the design, which may involve creating a new, more refined model.

flashcards.back: A 2D or 3D representation of a design on paper or a screen. Examples include sketches, orthographic projections, and perspective drawings. They are used to visualise and communicate ideas.
A tangible, three-dimensional representation of a design. It can be scaled or full-size. Examples include clay models, foam mock-ups, and 3D printed prototypes.
A digital model created using Computer-Aided Design (CAD) software. These models can be 2D drawings or 3D solid/surface models, and are easily modified and can be used for simulation.

flashcards.front: Differentiate between a mock-up and a prototype.
flashcards.back: A mock-up is a scale or full-size model used to test aesthetic and ergonomic aspects, but it is not functional. A prototype is a model that simulates or incorporates the intended function, used for performance testing.

flashcards.front: What is 'fidelity' in the context of modelling?
flashcards.back: Fidelity refers to the degree to which a model represents the appearance and/or function of the final product. Low-fidelity models are simple and abstract (e.g., a block of foam), while high-fidelity models are very detailed and realistic (e.g., a working prototype).
They are quick and inexpensive to produce, making them ideal for exploring multiple ideas in the early stages of the design process.
They can be time-consuming and costly to produce, especially high-fidelity prototypes. They may also be difficult to modify once built.
They are easily modified and can be used for complex simulations (like FEA or CFD) and generating manufacturing data (CAM), reducing errors and development time.

A computer-based (CAD) simulation method for predicting how a product reacts to real-world forces, vibration, heat, and other physical effects. It helps determine if a design will break, wear out, or work as intended.

A 2D graphical modelling technique that shows an object from different angles, typically front, top, and side views. It provides accurate dimensions for manufacturing but can be hard to visualise for non-technical stakeholders.

A 3D graphical modelling technique where the object is drawn with the axes at 120 degrees to each other. It provides a pictorial view that is easy to understand while maintaining some dimensional accuracy.

flashcards.front: What is a common trap when discussing models in an exam?
flashcards.back: Stating an advantage (e.g., 'CAD is accurate') without linking it to the specific design context provided in the question. Always justify *why* that advantage is relevant to the problem.

faq.q: What is the main difference between a mock-up and a prototype?
faq.a: The key difference is function. A mock-up is a non-functional model used to evaluate aesthetics and ergonomics (how it looks and feels). A prototype is a functional model, even if only partially, used to test how the product works.

faq.q: Can a 3D print be a mock-up and a prototype?
faq.a: Yes, it depends on its purpose. If you 3D print a solid block in the shape of a phone to test how it feels in your hand, it's a mock-up. If you 3D print a case that includes electronics and a working screen to test the software, it's a prototype.

faq.q: When would a simple sketch be better than a detailed CAD model?
faq.a: In the very early stages of ideation. When you want to generate a large number of different ideas quickly and cheaply, sketching is far more efficient. It allows for creative freedom without the technical constraints and time investment of creating a detailed CAD model for every single idea.

No. 'Scale' refers to the size of the model in relation to the final product (e.g., 1:10 scale model of a car). 'Fidelity' refers to the level of detail and realism of the model. You can have a low-fidelity, full-scale model (e.g., a car-sized block of foam) or a high-fidelity, scaled model (e.g., a detailed 1:10 model with working lights).

sections.content: Modelling is a fundamental activity in design technology, acting as the bridge between abstract ideas and tangible products. Designers use models to explore, refine, test, and communicate their concepts throughout the design cycle. Understanding the strengths and weaknesses of different modelling types is essential for making informed decisions that save time, reduce cost, and ultimately lead to better designs.

Graphical models are two-dimensional or three-dimensional visual representations. They are often the first step in externalising an idea. Freehand sketches are used for rapid idea generation, while more formal drawings like orthographic projections and isometric drawings are used for communicating precise information about form and dimension. Perspective drawings are used to create realistic impressions of what the final product will look like in its environment.

items: **Advantages:** Quick to produce, low cost, excellent for communicating initial ideas and aesthetics, useful for exploring a wide range of concepts rapidly.
**Disadvantages:** Can be ambiguous, lack of tactile feedback, difficult to test functionality, formal drawings require specific skills (e.g., technical drawing).

sections.content: Physical models are tangible objects that allow designers and users to interact with a design in a real-world space. They range from low-fidelity mock-ups, made from materials like foam or cardboard to test ergonomics and scale, to high-fidelity, functional prototypes that are used to test performance and mechanics. The choice of material and construction method depends entirely on what aspect of the design needs to be tested.

items: **Prototypes:** Test functionality and performance. Can be a subsystem or a full system.
**Advantages:** Provide tactile feedback, allow for user testing (ergonomics), can reveal unforeseen problems, help to communicate ideas to non-technical stakeholders.
**Disadvantages:** Can be expensive and time-consuming, modifications can be difficult, may not be durable, scaling can sometimes hide issues or create new ones.

sections.question: A design team is developing a new handheld games console for children aged 5-8. Justify the use of a series of physical mock-ups during the early stages of development. [4 marks]
sections.solution:
The solution should demonstrate an understanding of the user group and the purpose of mock-ups.

*   **[1] Identify context:** The target users are young children, for whom ergonomics and durability are critical design factors.
*   **[1] Explain benefit of physical model:** A physical mock-up allows the design team to test the ergonomics directly with the children. They can observe how the children hold the device, reach the controls, and handle its weight and size.
*   **[1] Explain benefit of mock-up over other models:** This tactile feedback is impossible to obtain from a graphical or CAD model. It provides qualitative data on comfort and usability.
*   **[1] Justify 'series' of mock-ups:** Using a series of low-fidelity mock-ups (e.g., from modelling foam) is a rapid and low-cost way to iterate. The team can quickly make adjustments to the form based on observation and feedback, refining the shape before committing to more expensive prototypes.

sections.content: CAD involves using specialist software to create 2D or 3D models. 3D CAD is particularly powerful, allowing for both 'solid modelling' (defining objects by their volume) and 'surface modelling' (defining objects by their exterior surfaces). These digital models are not just visual; they contain a wealth of data that can be used for analysis, simulation, and manufacturing. For example, Finite Element Analysis (FEA) can simulate structural loads, and Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) can simulate airflow over a surface. Once finalised, CAD data can be sent directly to Computer-Aided Manufacture (CAM) machines like 3D printers or CNC mills.

items: **Advantages:** High fidelity and accuracy, easy to modify and iterate, enables simulation (FEA, CFD) to test performance digitally, facilitates remote collaboration, data can be used directly for manufacturing (CAM).

**Disadvantages:** High initial cost for software and powerful hardware, requires significant training and skill, can be time-consuming to create complex models, lacks physical feedback for ergonomic testing.

sections.content: In Paper 2, you will often be asked to compare or justify the choice of a model. The key to a high-scoring answer is context. Do not just list generic pros and cons. Always link your points directly to the specific product, user, or scenario given in the question. For example, instead of saying 'physical models are good for feedback', say 'a physical mock-up of the kettle handle allows for direct ergonomic testing with elderly users to ensure a safe and comfortable grip'.

sections.question: A company is designing a new bicycle helmet. Compare the use of a CAD model with FEA simulation against a physical prototype for impact testing. [6 marks]
sections.solution:
A balanced comparison is required, addressing both methods.

**CAD Model with FEA:**
*   **[1] Advantage:** Allows for non-destructive testing. The same digital model can be subjected to hundreds of simulated impacts at different angles and forces without being destroyed, which is cost-effective.
*   **[1] Advantage:** Variables can be precisely controlled and isolated. The simulation can pinpoint exact stress concentrations and failure points in the material structure, providing detailed quantitative data.
*   **[1] Disadvantage:** The accuracy of the simulation is entirely dependent on the quality of the input data (e.g., material properties) and the assumptions made in the model. It may not perfectly replicate complex real-world fracture mechanics.

**Physical Prototype:**
*   **[1] Advantage:** Provides real-world validation. It tests the actual product, including any manufacturing imperfections, and gives definitive proof of performance under specific test conditions (e.g., meeting a safety standard).
*   **[1] Disadvantage:** Testing is destructive, meaning a new expensive prototype is needed for each test, making it a costly process.
*   **[1] Disadvantage:** While it shows *that* it failed, it can be difficult to determine the exact *why* and *where* the failure initiated without complex sensor equipment, unlike the detailed data from FEA.
